title: Run within-width stratified correlations for H3
description: Compute Pearson r between absorption_score and {sparse_probing, SCR, RAVEL} separately within each width tier (16k-only, 65k-only, 1M-only). The current correlation is confounded by width: all 5 high-absorption SAEs in C3B are 1M, all 5 low are 16k/65k. If r approaches zero within tiers, the entire H3 claim is an ecological fallacy. Also recompute partial correlations using width as a 3-level categorical dummy (16k/65k/1M) instead of log(width).

title: Fix abstract and conclusion 92.3% framing
description: The abstract presents '92.3% of letter features as showing some absorption' with a subordinate caveat. Replace the headline figure with validated rates: Type I strict = 3.8%, Chanin any-absorption = 80.8%. Present 92.3% only as an upper bound. Same fix needed in conclusion. The paper's own suspicious_flags say this rate is 'CRITICAL[ly] likely inflated.'

title: Replace 'validated quality indicator' with 'quality-correlated metric'
description: The abstract and conclusion use 'validated quality indicator' to describe the H3 finding. Correlational evidence cannot validate -- it can only show association. Replace throughout with 'quality-associated metric' or 'quality-correlated metric'. Add explicit note that causal evidence requires intervention experiments.

title: Diagnose SCR suppressor effect
description: SCR partial r jumps from -0.431 to -0.677 after controlling for {log_width, layer, arch_class}. Report sequential partial correlations: (1) control for log_width only, (2) add layer, (3) add arch_class. Identify which covariate produces the suppressor effect. If it is width, this further undermines the causal interpretation.

title: Move safety probe (Table 6) to appendix
description: The safety probe experiment (C3C) has n=3 SAEs, n=100 prompts, and 3+ confounds. It provides no evidence for the absorption-safety relationship. Move Table 6 to an appendix, reduce the in-text discussion to a brief paragraph noting the attempt was confounded and underpowered.

title: Report LV detector ROC-AUC < 0.5 prominently
description: ROC-AUC = 0.148 means the LV detector anti-predicts absorption (worse than random). This is more informative than F1 = 0.128. Discuss the mechanistic reason: absorbed parents have suppressed f_i, so alpha_ij is paradoxically low for absorbed pairs. The first alpha_ij bin [0, 0.1] shows absorption rate 0.848, confirming the inversion.

title: Report oracle F1 within pre-filtered set for H1
description: The decoder cosine pre-filter (cos > 0.15) imposes a 34% recall ceiling. Compute: (1) the recall achievable within the filtered set assuming perfect alpha_ij classification, and (2) F1 within the filtered set only. This disambiguates pre-filter failure from alpha_ij discrimination failure.

title: Move model-mismatch caveat to Section 1
description: The paper uses GPT-2 Small for H1/H2/H4/taxonomy and Gemma for H3. This is currently buried in Section 7.5. Move the caveat to Section 1 so readers understand the cross-model gap before encountering results.

title: Specify SAELens version and sae-spelling version
description: Neither SAELens nor sae-spelling version is pinned. Add version numbers to Section 4.1 or a requirements file.

title: Clarify DAS sample size discrepancy
description: Methodology claims 10,000 tokens for DAS estimation; actual results show n_word_act_samples = 40 per letter. Clarify whether DAS used 40 activation samples or the methodology's specified 10,000. If 40, add bootstrap CIs on DAS estimates and note the discrepancy.

title: Revise paper title
description: Current title 'When Features Compete' implies the competition framework works. Consider: 'Feature Absorption Predicts SAE Quality But Resists Unsupervised Detection' or similar.

title: Remove unsupported JumpReLU speculation in Section 2.2
description: The claim 'We hypothesize this is consistent with longer training amplifying the sparsity-gradient dynamics that produce absorption' is untested speculation. Remove or qualify with 'one untested possibility is...'
